coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted portable swabs for blood glucose management: A point-of-use assay for dual-mode visual detection based on bifunctional carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Ziwen
Zhong, Jiali
Tang, Xiaomin
Peng, Zoujun
Xu, Peng
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Controlling glucose (Glu) intake is a “required course” for diabetics, thus quickly and precisely measuring the amount of Glu in food is crucial. For this purpose, a novel smartphone-assisted portable swab for the dual-mode visual detection of Glu was constructed combined the selectivity of natural enzymes with the controllable catalytic activity of nanozymes. Glu was specifically decomposed by glucose oxidase (natural enzyme) to produce H2O2, which was catalyzed by carbon dots (FeMn/N-CDs, nanozyme) to accelerate the reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD, colorless) to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, yellow). As a result, the absorbance at 450 nm gradually increased with the increasing concentration of Glu, leading to a color change in the system from colorless to yellow. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of FeMn/N-CDs gradually decreased at 450 nm, while the fluorescence of DAP gradually increased at 550 nm, allowing for both ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection. Furthermore, natural enzyme and nanozyme together with OPD were co-loaded on the swabs to achieve cascade catalysis of Glu. The assembled portable swabs have detection ranges of 1–600 μM (LOD = 0.37 μM) and 4–1200 μM (LOD = 1.19 μM) for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection, respectively. The field test results on real samples demonstrated that the portable swabs have great promise for use in efficiently and accurately guiding the dietary intake of diabetics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multifunctional swabs
FeMn/N-CDs
Glucose
On-site detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Glu in real samples
Visualizing Glu utilizing a homemade swab-assisted dual-mode sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic process of FeMn/N-CDs. (b, c) The TEM images of FeMn/N-CDs. (d) Size distribution of FeMn/N-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of FeMn/N-CDs. (b) XPS spectra of FeMn/N-CDs High-resolution XPS spectra of FeMn/N-CDs: C 1s (c), N 1s (d), Mn 2p (e), and Fe 2p (f).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra for FeMn/N-CDs with excitation wavelengths from 310 to 400 nm. Schematic illustration of POD-like activity of FeMn/N-CDs: UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD (b), TMB (c) and ABTS (d). Oxidation catalyzed by FeMn/N-CDs in the presence of different concentration of H2O2 (e) and OPD (g), and corresponding double reciprocal diagram (f, h).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP and fluorescence emission spectra of FeMn/N-CDs (b) The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of FeMn/N-CDs with and without DAP. (c) The Zeta potentials of FeMn/N-CDs and DAP.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation of 370 nm with different concentrations of Glu (0.1–800 μM) (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon UV-light). (b) The linear relationship between Glu concentrations and I 550/I 450. (c) UV–vis spectroscopy with different concentrations of Glu (0.2–400 μM). (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon sunlight) (d) The linear relationship between Glu concentrations with ΔA at 450 nm. (e, f) Fluorescence and colorimetric selective detection Glu. (The concentration of Glu was 50 μM, while the conentrations of AA, PDA, GSH, and Cys were 0.5 mM, 2 mM, 1 mM, and 1 mM. The concentration of other interfering substances was 5 mM).

The color changes of multifunctional swabs under sunlight (a) and UV-light (c) with different Glu (0.5–1600 μM). (b) The linear relationship between Glu (1–600 μM) concentrations and G/B under sunlight. (d) The linear relationship between Glu (4–1200 μM) concentrations and G/B under UV-light.
Schematic diagram of the mechanism for detecting Glu based on FeMn/N-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the mechanism for detecting Glu based on multifunctional portable swab.
Comparison of various methods for Glu determination.
Fluorescence and colorimetry determination of Glu in human serum (n = 3).
Multifunctional portable swab determination of Glu in common food (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted portable swabs for blood glucose management: A point-of-use assay for dual-mode visual detection based on bifunctional carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Advanced Theranostic Materials and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Controlling glucose (Glu) intake is a “required course” for diabetics, thus quickly and precisely measuring the amount of Glu in food is crucial. For this purpose, a novel smartphone-assisted portable swab for the dual-mode visual detection of Glu was constructed combined the selectivity of natural enzymes with the controllable catalytic activity of nanozymes. Glu was specifically decomposed by glucose oxidase (natural enzyme) to produce H2O2, which was catalyzed by carbon dots (FeMn/N-CDs, nanozyme) to accelerate the reaction of o-phenylenediamine (OPD, colorless) to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, yellow). As a result, the absorbance at 450 nm gradually increased with the increasing concentration of Glu, leading to a color change in the system from colorless to yellow. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of FeMn/N-CDs gradually decreased at 450 nm, while the fluorescence of DAP gradually increased at 550 nm, allowing for both ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection. Furthermore, natural enzyme and nanozyme together with OPD were co-loaded on the swabs to achieve cascade catalysis of Glu. The assembled portable swabs have detection ranges of 1–600 μM (LOD = 0.37 μM) and 4–1200 μM (LOD = 1.19 μM) for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection, respectively. The field test results on real samples demonstrated that the portable swabs have great promise for use in efficiently and accurately guiding the dietary intake of diabetics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As the major energy source and metabolic intermediary in living organisms, glucose (Glu) is essential for human health [1]. The human body needs a suitable blood Glu range of 4.4–6.6 mM to sustain daily functions [2]. Prolonged hyperglycemia can have detrimental effects, including decreased immune system function, metabolic disturbances, and possibly diabetes. Similarly, persistently low Glu levels might aggravate the cardiovascular system and cause harm to brain tissue [3]. Therefore, it is quite critical to develop a method that can rapidly, simply, and accurately measure Glu.

Numerous techniques, including colorimetry [4], fluorescence spectrometry [5], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [6], chemiluminescence [7], and electrochemical method [8], have been published thus far for the detection of Glu. The colorimetric method is preferred due to its simplicity of equipment and quick detection time [9]. Fluorescence spectrometry, especially ratiometric fluorescence spectrometry has drew increasing attention due to its advantages of high sensitivity and good accuracy [10]. By measuring two light signals at the same excitation wavelength to calculate their relative changes, ratio fluorescence spectrometry has the potential to mitigate the impact of extraneous environmental influences and enhance the accuracy and sensitivity [11]. Therefore, a dual-mode detection strategy that combines ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric methods can be created to take use of their individual advantages for accurate detection of Glu. Though the aforementioned methods are more suitable for professional institutions, it still falls short of meeting the standards for home testing due to the expensive cost of the equipment and the demand for professional operation methods [12].

Cotton swab is a kind of inexpensive, simple, and convenient daily item, making them good carriers for designing sensors for portable sensing analysis [13]. For instance, Kandasamy et al. [14] employed cotton swabs to quickly identify pathogenic bacteria visually, while Zourob's team [15] developed lactoferrin-immobilized cotton swabs for the specific detection of foodborne pathogens in environmental samples. Most of the reported portable sensors equipped with swabs at present only loaded stable materials. However, for reaction systems involving unstable substances (chromogenic agents and enzymes), exogenous addition is still necessary to achieve immediate on-site detection. Therefore, effectively encapsulating these compounds in the swabs and releasing them during detection progress has become a research challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs) have a wide range of sensing applications due to their unique fluorescence and controllable catalytic properties [16]. The introduction of transition metal elements including cobalt, iron, and manganese, can significantly improve the enzyme-like activities of CDs, which enhances the sensing performance of optical biosensors and broadens their potential applications [17]. In this work, iron, cobalt, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FeMn/N-CDs) with excellent peroxidase-like activity were synthesized using the traditional one-pot hydrothermal method. Subsequently, a dual-mode biosensor (ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry) with outstanding performance based on the synthesized CDs was constructed to achieve rapid and accurate analysis of Glu levels. To selectively oxidize Glu to H2O2, the glucose oxidase (GOx) was employed. Following the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) from the catalytic H2O2 by FeMn/N-CDs, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) combined singlet oxygen to produce 3,3′-diaminobenzidine (DAP). This caused the colorless solution to become yellow, and the resultant DAP showed a noticeable UV absorption peak at 420 nm. Additionally, the generated DAP also exhibited a distinct fluorescence signal at 550 nm, which was capable of quenching the characteristic fluorescence peak (450 nm) of FeMn/N-CDs through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, for more practical quick Glu detection, an encapsulated multifunctional swab colorimetric sensing system with smartphone assistance was devised and constructed. The swabs were assembled by combining a cotton head loaded with FeMn/N-CDs and a hollow plastic rod encapsulating GOx and OPD. At the point of use, the blue circle was broken to release the contents, and the cascade catalysis reaction was then completed on the swab head. According to the obtained calibration curve, the color parameters (RGB values) of the swab tip are recognized by the smartphone and converted to Glu levels. This dual-mode sensing platform achieves accurate and rapid on-site detection of Glu without the requirement for lab equipment, expanding the practical application prospects of nanosensors and offering new ideas for the design of portable sensors.

The detailed synthesis procedure of FeMn/N-CDs was depicted in Fig. 1 a. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was first employed to characterize their morphology, and the results were shown in Fig. 1b–c. The FeMn/N-CDs were uniformly distributed in a spherical shape, with an average particle size of 2.3 nm (Fig. 1d). The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1c and Fig. S1a) revealed that the FeMn/N-CDs exhibited an ordered lattice spacing of 0.23 nm stripes, corresponding to the (100) crystalline plane of graphitic carbon [18].

The functional groups of FeMn/N-CDs were analyzed by FTIR. The FTIR spectra revealed (Fig. 2 a) absorption bands at 3450 cm−1 and 2980 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H groups and the C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [19]. The absorbance band at 1635 cm−1 was corresponded to the CO stretching vibration [20], while the absorbance band at 1403 cm−1 was corresponded to the CC vibration [21]. The absorption bands at 1350 and 1041 cm−1 indicated the C–N and C–O vibrations in FeMn/N-CDs [19], respectively. In addition, the weak absorption peaks appearing at 1000-500 cm−1 may correspond to the Fe–N [22] bond and Mn–N [23]. The elemental composition of FeMn/N-CDs was further analyzed employing XPS. As shown in Fig. 2b, the spectrum of FeMn/N-CDs involved the following peaks: C 1s (284.4 eV), N 1s (399.5 eV), O 1s (531.1 eV), Mn 2p (641.1 eV), and Fe 2p (710.6 eV). According to the C 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2c), the binding energies corresponding to C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO were 284.8, 285.7, 287.4, and 288.4 eV, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed three peaks at 399.3, 400.1, and 401.2 eV, which correspond to pyridinic N, pyrrole N, and graphitic N, respectively [24]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) was classified into CO (531.2 eV) and C–O (533.1 eV) [25]. In the Mn 2p spectrum (Fig. 2e), the peaks at 641.3 eV, 653.1 eV and 642.8 eV, 654.3 eV were associated with Mn2+ and Mn3+, respectively, while 645.5 eV and 656.9 eV corresponded to the two satellite peaks of Mn [26]. The Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 2f) at 713.7 eV, 727.8 eV and 710.7 eV, 724.7 eV peaks corresponded to the characteristic peaks of Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively, while the peak at 717.1 eV was identified as the satellite peak of Fe. The synergistic effect of the two redox pairs (Mn3+/Mn2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+) was a possible reason for the excellent peroxidase-like activity of FeMn/N-CDs [27]. The above findings indicated the effective synthesis of FeMn/N-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was first used to identify the optical property of FeMn/N-CDs. Fig. S1c showed obvious absorption peaks at 382 nm, and the Tyndall effect can be clearly observed when irradiating by a red laser, which further indicated FeMn/N-CDs were uniformly dispersed in an aqueous solution. Bright blue luminescence was readily seen under UV (365 nm) excitation (Fig. S1d). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the emission wavelength was progressively red-shifted when the excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 400 nm. This excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is consistent with the properties of the majority of carbon nanomaterials [28]. When the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, FeMn/N-CDs showed the strongest fluoresce, with a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm.

The POD-like activity of FeMn/N-CDs was confirmed using the classical chromogenic agent OPD. As seen in Fig. 3b–d distinct characteristic absorption peak appeared at 450 nm when FeMn/N-CDs, OPD, and H2O2 were present simultaneously compared to the control groups. 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 2,2′- azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) were used for comparison with OPD in the same conditions. Both visual inspection and spectral analysis showed that FeMn/N-CDs oxidized TMB and ABTS as efficiently as OPD (Fig. 3c and d).

The mechanism of FeMn/N-CD catalytic reactions can be studied by pointing out the particular kinds of reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by the decomposition of H2O2. The radical scavengers isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium benzoate (NaA), and tryptophan (Try) were supplemented to the POD-like reaction system, and the changes in absorbance at 450 nm were monitored. Fig. S2a showed that the absorbance values decreased significantly in the presence of Try compared to other groups. The results indicated that H2O2 was mainly decomposed by FeMn/N-CDs peroxidase to produce 1O2, which drove the oxidation of OPD to DAP. Based on the mechanism, the steady-state kinetic experiments were conducted to further investigate the POD-like activity of FeMn/N-CDs.

Specifically, the curves of absorbance versus time were obtained by keeping the constant concentration of one substrate and varying the concentration of the other. The Michaelis-Menten curve and the Lineweaver-Burk plot were employed to calculate substrate affinity (K
m ) and maximum reaction rate (V max), which were important parameters for assessing enzymatic activity. Lower values of K
m and larger values of V max imply a higher binding affinity for the substrate and a faster reaction rate. As can be seen from Fig. 3e and 3f, when OPD was used as the chromogenic substrate, the K
m and V max values were calculated to be 0.098 mM and 2.99 × 10−7 M s−1. When the concentration of H2O2 was kept constant, the two key parameters were found to be K
m = 0.27 mM and V max = 1.77 × 10−7 M s−1 (Fig. 3g and 3h). In contrast to the other nanozymes, as shown in Table S1, FeMn/N-CDs had lower K
m values, indicating that FeMn/N-CDs had a good affinity for substrates. In summary, FeMn/N-CDs exhibited well POD-like activity.

The dual-mode sensing mechanism for detecting Glu based on FeMn/N-CDs was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Glu was specifically decomposed by GOx to produce H2O2, which was catalyzed by FeMn/N-CDs to generate singlet oxygen. Subsequently, OPD was oxidized by molecular oxygen to form DAP, and the absorbance at 450 nm gradually increased with the rise in Glu concentration, leading to a color change from colorless to yellow in the system. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of FeMn/N-CDs gradually decreased at 450 nm, while the fluorescence of DAP gradually increased at 550 nm. A strong linear relationship was observed between the I
550 /I
450 ratio and the Glu concentration, causing the fluorescence of solution to shift from blue to yellow when exposed to the UV light.

In general, the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching include static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photogenerated electron transfer (PET), surface energy transfer (SET), Dexter energy transfer (DET), and internal filtering effect (IFE) [29]. To explore the interaction mechanism of FeMn/N-CDs with DAP, the fluorescence emission spectra of FeMn/N-CDs were analyzed. The emission spectra of FeMn/N-CDs at 450 nm largely overlapped with the UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP at 450 nm (Fig. 4 a), suggesting that the fluorescence quench might be caused by IFE and FRET. Fluorescence lifetime and zeta potential are important parameters for distinguishing between IFE and FRET [19]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence lifetime of FeMn/N-CDs decreased from 5.35 ns to 4.41 ns when DAP was present in the system, indicating that there was an energy transfer between FeMn/N-CDs and DAP. So the static quenching process of IFE was eliminated. The zeta potentials (Fig. 4c) of FeMn/N-CDs and DAP were further measured to be −16.09 mV and 18.32 mV, respectively. The opposite charges between them could form electrostatic attraction, which reduced the distance and enhanced energy transfer [30]. Therefore, the interaction between FeMn/N-CDs and DAP was arised from FRET.

Experiments were conducted to investigate the impact of relevant factors in the system and assess the feasibility of the dual-mode biosensor for Glu detection. It can be observed from fluorescence spectra (Fig. S2b) that the fluorescence curves remained almost unchanged when FeMn/N-CDs were combined with other substances under the same conditions. However, the fluorescence peak at 450 nm was significantly quenched and a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 550 nm only when FeMn/N-CDs, Glu, GOx, and OPD coexisted in the system. Similarly, in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S2c), the characteristic absorption peak of DAP at 450 nm was significantly increased only when FeMn/N-CDs, Glu, GOx, and OPD were added to the system. This phenomenon indicated the method can be successfully applied to the detection and analysis of Glu.

In order to enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, we examined the impact of pH, temperature, time, OPD concentration, GOx concentration, and CFeMn/N-CDs on the specific fluorescence (I
550 /I
450
) during catalysis. As shown in Figs. S2d and S2e, the data indicated that the I
550 /I
450 ratio increased with pH and the concentration of FeMn/N-CDs, reaching the maximum at pH = 4.8 and CFeMn/N-CDs = 50 μg/mL, and then decreased. The effect of temperature was explored, which was found that the I
550 /I
450 ratio gradually increased with rising temperature, stabilizing at 45 °C (Fig. S2f). According to the description in Fig. S2g, the I
550 /I
450 ratio basically reached a plateau at 20 min, with an OPD concentration of 10 mM and a GOx concentration of 5 μg/mL (Figs. S2h and i). Therefore, the optimal conditions, pH = 4.8, CFeMn/N-CDs = 50 μg/mL, temperature of 45 °C, duration of 20 min, 6 mM OPD, and GOx concentration of 5 μg/mL were chosen for the subsequent experiments, during which FeMn/N-CDs demonstrated outstanding POD-like catalytic performance.

The biosensor was constructed under the optimal experimental conditions for sensitive and accurate detection of Glu. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the I
550 /I
450 increased linearly with the rise in Glu concentration within the range of 0.1–800 μM. The linear equation was I
550 /I
450 = 3.844 CGlu + 0.308 (R2 = 0.998), based on calculated the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.041 μM. For the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5c and d), the linear relationship between absorbance at 450 nm and Glu concentration in the range of 0.2–400 μM was given, and a linear regression equation can be expressed as ΔA = 3.188 CGlu + 0.0332 (R2 = 0.996) with a LOD of 0.073 μM. It is worth explaining that ΔA = A - A0, where A and A0 represent the absorbance values of the solution in the presence and absence of the assay, respectively. Table 1 summarized the data of the current system in comparison with other methods found in the literature [31–36]. The sensing platform for Glu detection based on FeMn/N-CDs offers a broad detection range, a short detection time, and a low detection limit. Therefore, the proposed sensor is sensitive, rapid, and reliable for the detection of Glu, which also provides an important basis for the subsequent development of portable swabs.

Selectivity is the important criteria for evaluating the effectiveness of the dual-mode sensor for detecting Glu, which was assessed by introducing different interfering substances into the detection system both in the presence and absence of Glu (50 μM). Figs. S3a and S3b illustrated that the interfering substances at 100-fold (5 mM) resulted in a significant reduction in the I
550 /I
450 value and the absorbance value at 450 nm, demonstrating the biosensor exhibited good selectivity for Glu. When the interfering substances coexisted with Glu under the same conditions, only AA, PDA, GSH, and Cys interfered with the detection system at this level. Fortunately, when their concentrations were narrowed down to AA (0.5 mM), PDA (2 mM), GSH (1 mM), and Cys (1 mM) (Figs. S3c and d), the effect could be overlooked (Fig. 5e and 5f). Actually, the typical levels of AA, PDA, GSH, and Cys in serum were around 7.8 μM, 1 μM, 3.4 μM, and 9.7 μM [37], respectively, which were significantly lower than those of human Glu. Therefore, the other substances did not significantly interfere with the results of the real sample determination. In conclusion, our proposed dual-mode sensor can sensitively and accurately detect Glu in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Glu in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability and accuracy of the sensor, fluorescence and UV–Vis analyses were performed on five serum samples. The corresponding optical images were shown in Figs. S4a and S4b. The color changes were visible to the naked eye. The results in Table 2 are consistent with those of the biochemical analyzer, indicating that the biosensor has high detection accuracy. The sensing performance was further verified through the standard addition method. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of the specific fluorescence method were 99.3–102.8 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 2.3 %. The recoveries of the UV–Vis absorption method were 98.1–104.4 % with RSD not more than 3.2 %. The results showed that the two detection modes have good recoveries and low relative errors, indicating that the dual-mode biosensor has good precision and significant potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualizing Glu utilizing a homemade swab-assisted dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve a convenient and fast Glu detection sensor at home, a portable multifunctional swab-based detection method was designed, as illustrated in Scheme 2 . The clear structure of the swab was shown in Fig. S5a. The FeMn/N-CDs were loaded at one end of the swab head, and the various reactants (OPD and GOx) were encapsulated in the swab sticks. The bright blue fluorescence indicated the successful loading of FeMn/N-CDs (Fig. S5b). Moreover, we used Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometry (EDS) with SEM to further validate the successful immobilization of FeMn/N-CDs on the swab head. Fig. S5c-l show the SEM images and the elemental mapping of the swab head. The elements Fe and Mn are uniformly dispersed around the fibers with C and O as the backbone. The swab heads unloaded with FeMn/N-CDs showed no Fe and Mn elements, and the elemental contents in Table S2 were highly consistent with the images, further demonstrating the successful immobilization of FeMn/N-CDs. The fabrication method and detailed operations are described in the supplementary information, or refer to Video 1, Supporting Information.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126545

The following are the Supplementary data related to this article.
Video 1
2
Video 1

RGB is the color standard that allows multiple colors to be obtained by changing three colors: red (R), green (G), and blue (B), and superimposing them on each other. The mobile application can be used to convert any color into its unique corresponding RGB value. Therefore, a smartphone-assisted method for Glu detection in combination with the assembled swabs was designed. We used a WeChat Color Fetch app to convert images into color block information and recorded their corresponding RGB values (Fig. S6). To evaluate the impact of immobilization on the sensing platform, we measured the kinetic parameters post-immobilization. When OPD was utilized as the chromogenic substrate, K
m and V max values were determined to be 0.091 mM and 2.74 × 10−7 M−1 s, respectively. Keeping the H2O2 concentration constant, the key parameters were found to be K
m = 0.25 mM and V max = 1.68 × 10−7 M−1 s (Figs. S7a-d). As shown in Table S3, K
m and V max exhibited minimal changes after immobilization compared to before immobilization, suggesting that the impact of immobilization on the dynamics of the sensing platform was insignificant.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, DAP concentration increased with increasing Glu concentration, accompanied by noticeable color changes. Under fluorescent light, the swab tip gradually changed from colorless to yellow (Fig. 6c). The colors were converted to their corresponding RGB values by a color recognizer. The results in Fig. 6b showed that G/B and Glu concentrations were linearly correlated in the concentration range of 1–600 μM, with a linear regression equation of Y = 0.00526CGlu + 1.208 (R2 = 0.992, LOD = 0.37 μM). When irradiated with UV light (365 nm), the swab tip gradually changed from blue to bright yellow. After converting the colors to the corresponding RGB values, the results revealed a linear correlation between G/B and Glu concentrations in the concentration range of 4–1200 μM. The linear regression equation was Y = 0.00265CGlu + 1.147 (R2 = 0.993, LOD = 1.19 μM) (Fig. 6d).

The first step in treating diabetes is to control Glu intake, which is the key component of food [38,39]. Therefore, accurate and convenient measurement of Glu content can guide the dietary intake of diabetic patients, which is beneficial for their treatment. Six common foods (fresh dates, grapes, apples, pears, lychees, and honey) were analyzed to assess the effectiveness of portable swabs in daily life and the precision of their outcomes. The corresponding sunlight and UV light irradiation images were shown in Figs. S8a and b. The results were essentially the same whether under sunlight or UV light. To further verify the detection performance, the standard addition method was used. As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries were 98.5–102.7 %, and the RSD was less than 3.7 % under sunlight. The recoveries of UV-light were 98.2–102.3 % with RSD not more than 3.4 %. The aforementioned reults showed that the portable swab has a good recovery rate and low relative error, indicating that it possesses good precision.

Batch stability and storage stability were examined to confirm the viability of portable swabs for field testing. After 30 days of storage, there was a slight decrease in relative activity, indicating that the swabs can maintain their original accuracy even after prolonged storage (Fig. S9a). Batch stability was assessed by calculating the G/B values of the swab head. The results (Fig. S9b) showed that all seven batches of the multifunctional swabs accurately detected Glu, indicating their high reproducibility. By calculating the cost of the sensing platform, the data in Table S4 showed that our designed sensing platform has a low cost per detection. In summary, the portable sensor has great practical application capabilities.

An efficient, rapid, and accurate dual-mode (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence) for Glu assay was established using GOx and FeMn/N-CDs combined with OPD. Meanwhile, the designed multifunctional portable swab dual-mode detection system, combined with smartphone readout, showed excellent performance in identifying Glu concentration, which significantly reduced costs and improved convenience. The experimental results showed that the method has good accuracy and precision for detecting Glu in real samples, which achieved point-of-use analysis of Glu. Therefore, the sensor has promising applications for guiding the dietary choices of diabetic patients and regulating their Glu intake in daily life.

The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from diabetics. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

Ziwen Liao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Jiali Zhong: Validation, Investigation. Xiaomin Tang: Resources, Investigation. Zoujun Peng: Writing – review & editing, Software, Data curation. Peng Xu: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ping Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099) and the open project program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (No. SKLF-KF-202416).
